user_query:
We’re prototyping a multi-actor, stateful writing assistant for our docs team. Please read the following inputs first and then implement exactly what’s specified:
- input/spec.yaml — the functional blueprint (roles, nodes, routing, exit conditions)
- input/rubric.md — acceptance rubric and required sections for documentation
- input/nonfunctional.txt — non-functional requirements, safety/approval guardrails

Deliverables (write all files under output/):
1) output/agent_graph.py — A Python implementation of a stateful, graph-based agent workflow that meets the spec. Requirements:
   - Define a typed shared state with chat/message history using an append-style reducer so new messages are aggregated rather than overwritten.
   - Implement nodes for Planner, Researcher (tool-calling), Writer, Editor, plus a Supervisor router that decides the next step dynamically.
   - Include a dedicated tool-execution node and conditional routing that loops until a clean exit condition (no infinite loops). Make sure tool execution routes back to the decision maker when appropriate.
   - Add a human-in-the-loop approval point that pauses the run before any external-tool action (approval required before proceeding).
   - Add persistence via a checkpointer so the run can be resumed.
   - Support both single-call invocation and event streaming execution patterns.
   - Keep any example tool usage non-destructive and stubbed (no real network calls).
2) output/README.md — Explain the architecture in clearly labeled sections that match input/rubric.md. Must include: State Schema & Reducers, Nodes & Responsibilities, Control Flow & Conditional Routing, Human-in-the-Loop Approval, Persistence/Checkpointing, Streaming & Event Semantics, and How to Run.
3) output/METACOGNITION.md — Document how a 5-stage metacognitive protocol is embedded into this workflow, with explicit sections for: Intent Decoding, Difficulty Assessment, Boundary Declaration, Execution Monitoring, Delivery Validation. Map each stage to concrete nodes/logic in the graph and show when checkpoints occur.
4) output/sample_runs.json — Two example inputs and expected flows: one that requires tool approval (requires_approval: true) and one that completes without tools (requires_approval: false). Include an ordered list of expected node transitions for each example.

Important:
- Read the inputs carefully and follow them closely. If anything is ambiguous, ask up to 3 targeted clarifying questions, then proceed.
- Do not include real external calls or side effects; tool calls should be stubbed and safely demonstrable.
- Use only relative paths (input/, output/).
